You are going to encounter a lot of Chinese classical texts like this. To understand them, you need to know a few things:

1. Most Chinese classical texts are written in ancient Chinese, which is very different from modern Chinese (Mandarin) both in writing system and vocabulary.
2. Many Chinese classical texts are written in a highly figurative style, and may not seem to make much sense if taken literally.
3. To understand a Chinese classical text, it is often necessary to have knowledge of the historical and cultural context in which it was written.

With that in mind, here is my attempt at a translation and interpretation of the text you provided:

---

"Though I tend the sheep at the pass, yet I learn the law by study. With the heart for the decisive agent, the river flows to the hall. With wisdom for the dark heaven, discuss error and think of the rites. With name for the capital, stop at the country side. If one has not heard of virtue, one has not thought of reality. Not thought of change, observe the decisive agent and do not understand the law. Pile up clouds far from righteousness, know the humane and investigate heaven. Think of water and establish the heart, discriminate heaven discriminate the people. If the hidden man does not attend to the state at the morning court, then think of principle. To name water, the small of virtue. Though the spring governs the world, yet use craft for governance. Like the flow of water, like the reasoning of things."

---

The first part of the text (lines 1-12) is spoken by a shepherd who, though his humble occupation would seem to be far removed from politics and governance, is in fact engaged in the study of law and principles. His "heart" is what moves the "decisive agent" (a Confucian term for something that brings about change), just as the river flows to the hall (i.e., the place where decisions are made). Wisdom is what allows us to understand the "dark heaven" (a metaphor for the unknown or unenlightened state of things) and to think of the rites, which are the principles by which we govern ourselves and others. Name (i.e., the capital) is where we stop in our quest for governance, at the country side (i.e., among the people). If we have not heard of virtue, we have not thought of reality; if we have not thought of change, we observe the decisive agent but do not understand the law. Pile up clouds far from righteousness (i.e., do not let distance or obstacles prevent you from pursuing righteousness), know the humane (a Confucian term for compassion and love for others) and investigate heaven (a Confucian term for the ultimate reality or principle of the universe). Think of water (a Confucian metaphor for the principle of constant change and flux) and establish the heart (i.e., make the principle of change and flux the basis of your decision-making), discriminate heaven (i.e., understand the principles of the universe) discriminate the people (i.e., apply those principles to govern the people). If the hidden man (i.e., the one who is not actively engaged in politics) does not attend to the state at the morning court (i.e., the place where decisions are made), then think of principle (i.e., return to the fundamental principles of governance). To name water, the small of virtue (i.e., the principle of change and flux is but a small part of virtue, which is something greater and more encompassing). Though the spring governs the world, yet use craft for governance (i.e., though principles are the ultimate basis of governance, craft and skill are also necessary). Like the flow of water, like the reasoning of things (i.e., the principle of change and flux is like the flow of water, and the reasoning of things is also subject to constant change and flux).
